Getting Started

Updated April 1, 2026 • 8 min read • Edit on GitHub

The web-fetch module provides a high-performance HTTP fetching pipeline designed for LLM-friendly content extraction. It handles SSRF protection, content sanitization, intelligent caching, and Markdown conversion in a single composable API.

Built on Node.js standard library primitives with zero external dependencies, this module prioritizes determinism and security over convenience. Every HTTP response passes through a multi-stage pipeline that strips navigation noise, removes injection attempts, and converts HTML to clean Markdown.

Installation

The module ships as part of the Project OS toolchain. No separate installation is required if you are using the standard project scaffold. For standalone usage, copy the tools/web-fetch/src/ directory into your project.

Node.js 22.6 or later is required for native TypeScript support via --experimental-strip-types. The cache layer requires Node 22 for the node:sqlite built-in module.

Quick Start

The primary entry point is fetchUrl, which accepts a URL, optional per-request options, and an optional configuration object. It returns a FetchResult containing the extracted Markdown content, metadata, and sanitization telemetry.

import { fetchUrl } from "./tools/web-fetch/src/pipeline.ts";

const result = await fetchUrl("https://docs.example.com/guide", {
  maxTokens: 8000,
  mode: "readable",
  noCache: false,
});

console.log(result.title);        // "Getting Started"
console.log(result.content);      // Markdown content
console.log(result.tokenEstimate); // ~2400
console.log(result.sanitized);    // ["html-comment", "dangerous-attr"]

Configuration

All pipeline behavior is controlled through a WebFetchConfig object. The default configuration is production-ready for most use cases, but you can override specific fields using a JSON config file or by passing a config object directly.

Field Default Description
fetch.timeout 15000 Request timeout in milliseconds
fetch.retryCount 3 Number of retry attempts on transient failures
cache.enabled true Enable SQLite-backed response cache
extraction.maxTokens 15000 Maximum output tokens before truncation

API Reference

The pipeline exports several lower-level functions for cases where you need fine-grained control. The validateUrl function performs SSRF checks independently, sanitizeHtml and sanitizeMarkdown can be applied to arbitrary content, and extractAndConvert handles HTML-to-Markdown conversion without network I/O.

For caching, the WebFetchCache class wraps the SQLite database with a content-addressed blob store. Cache entries include ETag and Last-Modified headers for conditional GET requests, which reduces bandwidth on subsequent fetches of the same resource.

Rate limiting is enforced per-domain using a token bucket algorithm. The default configuration allows two requests per second per domain. You can adjust this via rateLimit.defaultRps in the configuration.

Error Handling

All errors thrown by the pipeline are plain Error instances with descriptive messages. SSRF violations include the blocked hostname and reason. Blocked responses include the detection reason. Network errors preserve the original error message and code.

  • SSRF blocked: hostname is on the blocklist
  • Fetch blocked: challenge page detected at URL
  • HTTP 404: permanent failure fetching URL
  • DNS lookup failed: network or resolution error

Consumers should catch errors at the call site and handle them according to their retry policy. The pipeline itself retries transient HTTP errors (5xx, 429) internally up to retryCount times with exponential backoff.
